1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
147	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
148	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
149	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
150	  \[
151	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
152	  \]
153	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
154	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
155	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{theorem}
159	
160	\leanok
161	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
162	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
164	  
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
166	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
169	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
170	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
171	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
172	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
174	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
175	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
176	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
177	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
178	\end{theorem}
179	
180	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
181	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
182	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
183	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
184	% Theorem~REF by restricting to a non-empty
185	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
186	\begin{proof}
187	  
188	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
189	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
190	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
191	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). Theorem~REF
192	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
193	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
194	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
195	\end{proof}
196	
197	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
198	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
199	
200	\begin{theorem}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
204	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
206	  
207	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
208	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
209	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
210	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
212	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and let
213	  %  \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on the projective space \(\PP^n_S\)
214	  %  over \(S\). Then the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials of
215	  %  restrictions of \(\F\) to fibers of \(\PP^n_S\to S\)
216	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each \(f\in I\) there exist
217	  %  a locally closed subscheme \(S_f\) of \(S\),
218	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
219	  %
220	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set \(|S_f|\) of \(S_f\) consists of
221	  %  all points \(s\in S\) where the Hilbert polynomial of the
222	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_s\) is \(f\). In particular,
223	  %  the subsets \(|S_f|\subset |S|\) are disjoint,
224	  %  and their set-theoretic union is \(|S|\).
225	  %
226	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
227	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
228	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
229	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
230	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
231	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
232	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
233	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
234	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\).
235	  %
236	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
237	  %  Let the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
238	  %  ordering, defined by putting \(f<g\) whenever \(f(n) < g(n)\) for all
239	  %  \(n \gg  0\). Then the closure in \(S\) of the subset \(|S_f|\)
240	  %  is contained in the union of all \(|S_g|\) where \(f\le g\)."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
242	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
243	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on the relative
244	  projective space \(\PP^n_S\) over \(S\).  Then:
245	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
246	    \item The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
247	      \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is
248	      finite.
249	    \item For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme
250	      \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly
251	      those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\).  The collection \(\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}\) is
252	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\).  Forming the coproduct
253	      \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled
254	      from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \F\) on
255	      \(\PP^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this
256	      property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi^* \F\) on
257	      \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through \(i\).
258	      Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
259	    \item Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the
260	      closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
261	  \end{enumerate}
262	\end{theorem}
263	
264	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
265	the special case \(n = 0\) (when \(\PP^n_S = S\) and \(\F\) is just a coherent sheaf
266	on \(S\)), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
267	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
268	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
269	of \(\F(m)\) for \(m \gg 0\) on \(\F_{V_i}\) for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
270	isomorphisms for the direct images \(\pi_* \F(m)\), which allow comparing
271	strata for different twists.
272	
273	\begin{lemma}
274	[Flat locus on \(S\) for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
275	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
276	  
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
278	  % \(n=0\) (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
279	  % L1849-L1885).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "\textbf{Special case: } Let \(n=0\), so that \(\PP^n_S=S\).
282	  %  For any \(s\in S\), the \textbf{fiber} \(\F|_s\) of \(\F\) over \(s\)
283	  %  will mean the pull-back of \(\F\) to the subscheme
284	  %  \(\spec \kappa(s)\) [...]. The Hilbert polynomial of the
285	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to the fiber over \(s\) is the degree \(0\)
286	  %  polynomial \(e \in \Q[\lambda]\), where \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s\).
287	  %
288	  %  By Nakayama lemma, any basis of \(\F|_s\) prolongs to a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\)
289	  %  to give a set of generators for \(F|_U\). Repeating this argument,
290	  %  we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) in which
291	  %  there is a right-exact sequence
292	  %  ${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}
293	  %  \stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$.
294	  %  Let \(I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V\) be the ideal sheaf
295	  %  formed by the entries of the \(e\times m\) matrix
296	  %  \((\psi_{i,j})\) [...].
297	  %  Let \(V_e\) be the closed subscheme of \(V\) defined by \(I_e\).
298	  %  For any morphism of schemes
299	  %  \(f: T\to V\), the pull-back sequence [...]
300	  %  is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products.
301	  %  Hence the pull-back \(f^*\F\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module of
302	  %  rank \(e\) if and only if \(f^*\psi =0\), that is,
303	  %  \(f\) factors via the subscheme \(V_e\hra V\) defined by the vanishing of all
304	  %  entries \(\psi_{i,j}\).
305	  %  Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
306	  %
307	  %  As the rank of the matrix \((\psi_{i,j})\) is lower semi-continuous,
308	  %  it follows that the function \(e\) is upper semi-continuous, which
309	  %  proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
310	  %  when \(n=0\)."
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (special case \(n=0\) of the main theorem).}
312	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(\F\) be a coherent \(\OO_S\)-module.
313	  For each integer \(e \ge 0\), there exists a locally-closed subscheme
314	  \(S_e \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set is
315	  \(\{s \in S : \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s = e\}\). The family \(\{S_e\}_e\) is a
316	  finite, mutually disjoint, set-theoretically covering stratification of \(S\),
317	  and a morphism \(f : T \to S\) pulls back \(\F\) to a locally-free
318	  \(\OO_T\)-module of rank \(e\) if and only if \(f\) factors through \(S_e\).  The
319	  function \(s \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\) is upper-semicontinuous on \(S\).
320	\end{lemma}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  
324	  By [Nitsure], \S4 (verbatim above): for \(s \in S\), Nakayama produces a
325	  neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a right-exact local presentation
326	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\psi} \OO_V^{\oplus e} \to \F|_V \to 0\) where
327	  \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\).  The closed subscheme \(V_e \hookrightarrow V\)
328	  defined by the ideal sheaf generated by the matrix entries of \(\psi\)
329	  represents the locus where \(\F\) becomes locally free of rank \(e\): by
330	  right-exactness of tensor product, a base change \(f : T \to V\) has
331	  \(f^* \F\) locally free of rank \(e\) iff \(f^* \psi = 0\), iff \(f\) factors
332	  through \(V_e\).  Glueing the \(V_e\) over the cover of \(S\) by such
333	  neighbourhoods (and intersecting with \(V \setminus \bigcup_{e' > e} V_{e'}\)
334	  to get the strictly-equal-to-\(e\) stratum) produces the locally-closed
335	  \(S_e\). Upper-semicontinuity of \(e\) follows because the rank of \(\psi\) is
336	  lower-semicontinuous.
337	\end{proof}

502	  \(f(N+i)\)'' for all \(i \ge 0\).  These conditions are cut out by an increasing

590	\section{Application: relative curves}
591	\label{sec:flatstrat_curve_application}
592	
593	In the Route~A consumer setting, the input to the flattening-stratification
594	machinery is the pullback \(C \times_k T \to T\) where \(C\) is a smooth proper
595	curve over a field \(k\) and \(T\) is any \(k\)-scheme.  The hypotheses of
596	Nitsure's Theorem~REF demand
597	\emph{projective} relative dimension, not merely \emph{proper}; we record that
598	no extra work is required to instantiate the theorem in our setting.
599	
600	\begin{lemma}
601	[Smooth proper curve over \(k\) is projective]
602	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}
603	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6 (curve \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-projective;
604	  % proper \(\Rightarrow\) projective for curves), and standard consequences in
605	  % chapter III. The precise statement ``every smooth proper curve over a field
606	  % is projective'' is one of the classical equivalences for \(\dim 1\) and is
607	  % textbook material; this block is project-bespoke (no verbatim source
608	  % quote is reproduced here as the result is folklore-level and cited only
609	  % structurally for the Route~A reduction).
610	  \textit{Source: classical; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.6.7 \& II.7.6 (curves).}
611	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\).  Then \(C \to \Spec k\) is
612	  projective.  Consequently, for any noetherian \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the relative
613	  curve \(C \times_k T \to T\) is projective in the sense of Grothendieck (there
614	  exists a relatively very ample line bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\)).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{proof}
618	  Projectivity of a smooth proper curve over a field is classical
619	  ([Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6; alternatively: every divisor of large
620	  positive degree on a smooth proper curve \(C\) is very ample, providing the
621	  embedding \(C \hookrightarrow \PP^N_k\) for some \(N\)). Pulling the ample line
622	  bundle back along \(C \times_k T \to C\) produces a relatively very ample line
623	  bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\).
624	\end{proof}
625	
626	\begin{corollary}
627	[Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative curve]
628	  \label{cor:flattening_stratification_curve}
629	  
630	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a smooth proper curve over \(k\), and \(T\) a noetherian
631	  \(k\)-scheme.  Let \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(C \times_k T\). Then the
632	  flattening-stratification conclusion of
633	  Theorem~REF applies: there is a finite
634	  set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials and locally-closed subschemes
635	  \(T_f \hookrightarrow T\) for \(f \in I\), set-theoretically covering \(T\)
636	  disjointly, such that (a) \(\F|_{C \times_k T_f}\) is flat over \(T_f\) for
637	  each \(f\), and (b) any morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) such that
638	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(C \times_k T'\) is \(T'\)-flat factors uniquely through some
639	  \(T_f\).
640	\end{corollary}
641	
642	\begin{proof}
643	  
644	  By Lemma~REF, \(C \times_k T \to T\) is
645	  projective, hence factors through a closed immersion into \(\PP^n_T\) for some
646	  \(n \ge 0\).  Push \(\F\) forward along the closed immersion (extending by zero
647	  outside the image) to a coherent sheaf \(\tilde \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\); this
648	  operation preserves \(T\)-flatness in both directions because the closed
649	  immersion is a finite morphism.  Apply
650	  Theorem~REF to \(\tilde \F\) on
651	  \(\PP^n_T\) to obtain \(\{T_f\}\) with the stated universal property.
652	\end{proof}

63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
176	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
177	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
178	
179	### What you MUST do
180	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
181	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
182	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
183	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
184	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
185	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
186	
187	### What you MUST NOT do
188	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
189	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
190	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
191	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
192	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
193	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
194	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
195	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
196	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
197	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
198	
199	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
200	
201	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
202	
203	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
204	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
205	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
206	
207	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
208	
209	```latex
210	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
211	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
212	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
213	  \mathlibok
214	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
215	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
216	\end{lemma}
217	```
218	
219	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
220	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
221	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
222	
223	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
224	
225	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
226	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
227	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
228	
229	## Reading references
230	
231	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
232	
233	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
234	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
235	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
236	
237	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
238	
239	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
240	
241	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
242	
243	Conditions for dispatch:
244	
245	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
246	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
247	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
248	
249	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
250	
251	```
252	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
253	  --name reference-retriever \
254	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
255	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
256	  --write-domain 'references/**'
257	```
258	
259	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
260	
261	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
262	
263	## Workflow
264	
265	1. Read your directive completely.
266	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
267	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
268	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
269	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
270	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
271	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
272	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
273	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
274

\end{theorem}


\proof It is enough to prove the theorem for open subschemes of
$S$ which cover $S$, as
the resulting strata will then glue together by their universal property. 


\textbf{Special case: } Let $n=0$, so that $\PP^n_S=S$. 
For any $s\in S$, the \textbf{fiber} $\F|_s$ of $\F$ over $s$
will mean the pull-back of $\F$ to the subscheme
$\spec \kappa(s)$, where $\kappa(s)$ is the residue field at $s$. 
(This is obtained by tensoring the stalk of $\F$ at $s$ with the
residue field at $s$, both regarded as ${\mathcal O}_{S,s}$-modules.)
The Hilbert polynomial of the 
restriction of $\F$ to the fiber over $s$ is the degree $0$ 
polynomial $e \in \Q[\lambda]$, where $e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s$. 

By Nakayama lemma, any basis of $\F|_s$ prolongs to a neighbourhood $U$ of $s$ 
to give a set of generators for $F|_U$. Repeating this argument,
we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood $V$ of $s$ in which
there is a right-exact sequence
$${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e} 
\stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$$
Let $I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V$ be the ideal sheaf
formed by the entries of the $e\times m$ matrix
$(\psi_{i,j})$ of the homomorphism 
${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}$. 
Let $V_e$ be the closed subscheme of $V$ defined by $I_e$. 
For any morphism of schemes
$f: T\to V$, the pull-back sequence 
$${\mathcal O}_T^{\oplus m} \stackrel{f^*\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_T^{\oplus e} 
\stackrel{f^*\phi}{\to}f^* \F \to 0$$
is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products. 
Hence the pull-back $f^*\F$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module of
rank $e$ if and only if $f^*\psi =0$, that is, 
$f$ factors via the subscheme $V_e\hra V$ defined by the vanishing of all
entries $\psi_{i,j}$.
Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.


As the rank of the matrix $(\psi_{i,j})$ is lower semi-continuous,
it follows that the function $e$ is upper semi-continuous, which
proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
when $n=0$. 


\textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer $n$ to be arbitrary.
The idea of the proof is as follows:  
We show the existence of a stratification
of $S$ which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
stratifications for direct images $\pi_*\F(i)$ for all $i \ge N$ for some
integer $N$ (where the flattening
stratifications for $\pi_*\F(i)$ exist by case $n=0$ which we have 
treated above). This is the desired flattening stratification of $\F$
over $S$, as follows from Lemma \ref{graded module is flat}.


As $S$ is noetherian, it is a finite union of irreducible components,
and these are closed in $S$.
Let $Y$ be an irreducible component of $S$, and let $U$ be the
non-empty open subset of $Y$ which consists of all points which do not 
lie on any other irreducible component of $S$. 
Let $U$ be given the reduced subscheme
structure. Note that this makes $U$ an integral scheme, which is a
locally closed subscheme of $S$. 
By Theorem \ref{generic flatness} on generic flatness, 
$U$ has a non-empty open subscheme $V$ such that
the restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_V$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_V$.
Now repeating the argument with $S$ replaced by its reduced 
closed subscheme $S-V$,
it follows by noetherian induction on $S$ that there exist
finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
$V_i$ of $S$ such that set-theoretically 
$|S|$ is the union of the $|V_i|$ and 
the restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_{V_i}$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_{V_i}$.
As each $V_i$ is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows 
that only finitely many polynomials occur in $V_i$
in the family of Hilbert polynomials $P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$ 
as $s$ varies over points of $V_i$. This allows us to conclude 
the following:

\medskip

%\statement\label{finitely many Hilbert polynomials} 
\textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials 
$P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$ occur, as $s$ 
varies over all of $S$.

\medskip

By the semi-continuity theorem applied to the flat families 
$\F_{V_i} = \F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}$ para\-met\-rised by the 
finitely many noetherian schemes 
$V_i$, we get the following: 



\medskip
%\statement\label{uniform vanishing of higher cohomology} 
\textbf{(B) } There exists an integer $N_1$ such that 
$R^r\pi_*\F(m)=0$ for all $r\ge 1$ and $m\ge N_1$,
and moreover $H^r(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m)) = 0$
for all $s\in S$.


\medskip



For each $V_i$, by Lemma \ref{base change without flatness}
there exists an
integer $r_i \ge N_1$ with the property
that for any $m\ge r_i$ the base change homomorphism
$$(\pi_*\F(m))|_{V_i} \to {\pi_i}_*\F_{V_i}(m)$$ 
is an isomorphism, where $\F_{V_i}$ denotes the restriction of
$\F$ to $\PP^n_{V_i}$, and $\pi_i : \PP^n_{V_i}\to V_i$ the projection. 
As the higher cohomologies of all fibers (in particular, the first
cohomology) vanish by \textbf{(B)}, 
it follows by semi-continuity theory for the flat family $\F_{V_i}$
over $V_i$ that for any $s\in V_i$ 
the base change homomorphism
$$({\pi_i}_*\F_{V_i}(m))|_s \to  H^0(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$$
is an isomorphism for $m\ge r_i$. Taking $N$ to be the maximum of
all $r_i$ over the finitely many non-empty $V_i$, and composing the
above two base change isomorphisms, we get the following.


\medskip

%\statement\label{base change for direct image} 
\textbf{(C) } There exists an integer $N \ge N_1$ such that 
the base change homomorphism
$$(\pi_*\F(m))|_s \to  H^0(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$$
is an isomorphism for all $m\ge N$ and $s\in S$. 



\medskip


\textbf{Note } We now forget the subschemes $V_i$ but retain the facts 
\textbf{(A)}, %\ref{finitely many Hilbert polynomials},
\textbf{(B)}, %\ref{uniform vanishing of higher cohomology} 
\textbf{(C)} %\ref{base change for direct image} 
which were proved using the $V_i$.

\medskip


Let $\pi : \PP^n_S \to S$ denote the projection. Consider the 
coherent sheaves $E_0, \ldots, E_n$ on $S$, defined by
$$E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.$$
By applying the special case of of the theorem (where the relative dimension
$n$ of $\PP^n_S$ is $0$) to the sheaf $E_0$ on $\PP^0_S=S$, we get 
a stratification $(W_{e_0})$ of $S$
indexed by integers $e_0$, such that for any morphism 
$f: T\to S$ the pull-back $f^*E_0$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module 
of rank $e_0$ if and only if $f$ factors via $W_{e_0} \hra S$.

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "It is enough to prove the theorem for open subschemes of
% $S$ which cover $S$, as the resulting strata will then glue together by their
% universal property. [...] \textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer
% $n$ to be arbitrary. The idea of the proof is as follows: We show the
% existence of a stratification of $S$ which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
% stratifications for direct images $\pi_*\F(i)$ for all $i \ge N$ for some
% integer $N$ (where the flattening stratifications for $\pi_*\F(i)$ exist by
% case $n=0$ which we have treated above). This is the desired flattening
% stratification of $\F$ over $S$, as follows from Lemma REF."
% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1844-L1900; the
% full body L1844-L2090 is distributed across the sub-lemmas below, each of
% which carries its own verbatim % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF.)
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, lem:flat_locus_open, lem:nonflat_locus_proper,
    lem:noetherian_induction_strata, thm:generic_flatness}
  Following [Nitsure], \S4, the construction reduces to the special case
  \(n = 0\), assembled over the relative twists \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\).  As the
  argument is local on \(S\) (the strata glue by their universal property, part
  (ii)), and as the three conclusions are proved through the sub-lemmas below,
  we assemble them in the order (i), (ii), (iii).

  \emph{Part (i): finiteness of the Hilbert-polynomial set.}
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}, Noetherian induction on \(S\) (whose
  inductive step invokes generic flatness, Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness},
  on the integral locally-closed strata cut out by the irreducible-component
  decomposition of \(S\)) produces finitely many reduced, pairwise disjoint,
  locally-closed subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\) set-theoretically covering
  \(|S|\) on each of which \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) in
  the sense of Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  On a flat family the
  Hilbert polynomial \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is locally constant, so
  it takes finitely many values on each quasi-compact \(V_i\); the finite union
  over the finitely many \(V_i\) shows that the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
  occurring on \(S\) is finite.

  \emph{Part (ii): existence of the strata and their universal property.}
  Working over the flat pieces \(V_i\) of part (i), semicontinuity
  (cohomology-and-base-change) yields a uniform integer \(N\) such that, for all
  \(s \in S\) and \(m \ge N\), the higher cohomologies \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\)
  vanish for \(r \ge 1\) and the base-change homomorphism
  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism.  With this
  \(N\) fixed, Lemma~\ref{lem:noetherian_induction_strata} applies the \(n = 0\)
  stratification mechanism (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}) successively to the
  coherent sheaves \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\),
  refining stratum by stratum.  After \(n+1\) steps this produces a finite
  family of locally-closed subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\),
  universal for the property that each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free sheaf
  of constant rank \(e_i\); re-indexing the tuple \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\)
  gives the family \(\{W_f\}_{f \in I}\).  At any \(s \in W_f\) the base-change
  isomorphism and the cohomology vanishing force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has
  degree \(\le n\) and is determined by its values at \(N, \dots, N+n\)), so
  \(|W_f| = \{s : P_s = f\}\); these subsets are disjoint with union \(|S|\).
  The required locally-closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) is the closed
  subscheme cut out by the further local-freeness conditions
  ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all
  \(i \ge 0\)'' (a chain of coherent ideal sheaves that stabilises by
  Noetherianity), and \(\F|_{\PP^n_{S_f}}\) is flat over \(S_f\)
  (Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}) because the graded module
  \(\bigoplus_{i \gg 0} \pi_* \F(i)|_{S_f}\) is locally free.  Forming
  \(S' = \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the assembled morphism \(i : S' \to S\),
  the pullback \(i^* \F\) is \(S'\)-flat; and any \(\varphi : T \to S\) with
  \(\varphi^* \F\) flat over \(T\) has locally constant Hilbert polynomial, hence
  decomposes \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed loci where the polynomial
  is \(f\), each factoring uniquely through the corresponding \(S_f\) by the
  local-freeness universal property of Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}.  This
  gives the unique factorisation through \(i\), and shows each \(S_f\) is
  uniquely determined by \(f\).

  \emph{Part (iii): closure of strata.}
  Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\).  Since each
  \(S_f\) is a stratum of the \(n = 0\) flattening stratification applied to a
  single direct image \(\pi_* \F(p)\) for \(p \gg 0\) separating all polynomials
  in \(I\), the closure-of-strata assertion reduces to the upper-semicontinuity
  statement already established in the \(n = 0\) case
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}): the closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is
  contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
\end{proof}
